title: Arrays Are Isomorphic
description:
Given Two arrays a and b are isomorphic if we can replace each distinct value in a with a unique value so that a becomes b. Determine whether the two arrays are isomorphic. This requires a one-to-one mapping in both directions to avoid collisions.

    

Constraints:
    - 1 ≤ len(a), len(b) ≤ 10^5
- Arrays can contain any integers in 32-bit range
explanation:
Intuition
Isomorphism requires consistent forward and reverse mappings. We build two hash maps on the fly: from a→b and from b→a. Any mismatch in either direction proves the arrays are not isomorphic.

Approach
1. Initial Setup: Initialize two empty maps.
2. Main Algorithm: Walk the arrays in lockstep. Assign and validate the mappings a→b and b→a. If a conflict arises, return False.
3. Return Result: If the scan completes, the mapping is bijective and we return True.

Complexity Analysis
- Time Complexity: O(n) because the algorithm scans elements a constant
  number of times and performs O(1) work for each update or lookup.
- Space Complexity: O(n) due to the auxiliary structures required to
  store counts, prefix sums, or membership sets while ensuring fast access.

Key Insights
- Bijection implies both injective and surjective conditions
- Two maps capture both constraints in linear time

Common Pitfalls
- Only checking a→b allows collisions in b
- Forgetting to compare lengths first risks index errors
hints: You need a one-to-one mapping both ways
Track assignments while scanning in O(n)
Reject as soon as a conflict appears
